card.title: The Trolley Problem
card.teaser: One death to save five. Would you pull the lever? Would you push a stranger off a bridge? The difference might reveal how morality actually works.
card.coverImagePrompt: A vintage trolley speeding down a set of tracks that split in two, a lone figure standing at a lever in dramatic late-afternoon light, cinematic, high tension

lessons.title: Five lives or one
lessons.body: A runaway trolley is speeding down the tracks toward five workers who can't hear it coming. You're standing beside a lever. Pull it, and the trolley diverts onto a side track — where it will kill one person instead. Do nothing, and five die. Do something, and you've killed a stranger. What's the right choice, and why?

visual.imagePrompt: A tense aerial shot of trolley tracks splitting in a Y-shape, five workers on one branch, one worker on the other, a hand hovering over a lever

lessons.title: Foot's original puzzle
lessons.body: The thought experiment was invented in 1967 by British philosopher Philippa Foot, who used it to probe the ethics of abortion and the doctrine of double effect. A student named Judith Jarvis Thomson later added the now-famous twist: what if, instead of pulling a lever, you had to physically push a large stranger off a footbridge to stop the trolley? The math is identical — one death, five lives saved. But almost everyone who'd pull the lever refuses to push.

keyTerms.definition: A thought experiment comparing scenarios where sacrificing one life could save several
The ethical principle that harm caused as a side effect can be more permissible than harm intended as a means

visual.imagePrompt: Two illustrated panels side by side: one showing a person pulling a lever, one showing a person hesitating on a footbridge, stark comic-style art

lessons.title: Two moral traditions collide
lessons.body: Utilitarians, following Jeremy Bentham, say the right act is whatever produces the best outcome. Five lives beats one — pull the lever, push the man, don't flinch. Deontologists, following Immanuel Kant, say some acts are forbidden regardless of outcome: you cannot use a person as a mere means to an end. The trolley problem exposes how deeply most of us hold both intuitions at once, and how uncomfortable it is when they disagree.

keyTerms.definition: The ethical view that actions are right in proportion to how much well-being they produce
The ethical view that certain acts are right or wrong independent of their consequences

visual.imagePrompt: An old brass scale with a tiny figure on one side and five figures on the other, philosophical portraits of Bentham and Kant watching from the background

lessons.title: Your brain has two answers
lessons.body: Neuroscientist Joshua Greene ran fMRI studies of people working through both versions. The impersonal lever case activates regions associated with cool, deliberate reasoning. The push-from-the-bridge case lights up emotional centers — the same ones involved in direct harm. We seem to have two moral systems: a calculator and a conscience. The trolley problem forces them into the same room.
lessons.funFact: Self-driving car engineers now face real trolley problems in code — how to program a vehicle choosing between unavoidable collisions. MIT's 'Moral Machine' project collected 40 million decisions across 233 countries.

visual.imagePrompt: A split-screen brain scan, one half showing cool blue activation in reasoning areas, the other showing vivid red activation in emotional regions

lessons.title: The point isn't the answer
lessons.body: There is no 'correct' answer to the trolley problem — that's the point. It's a stethoscope, not a quiz. It lets you listen to the different moral voices inside yourself and notice where they disagree. Every time you decide how much a stranger's life is worth, how much means justify ends, how much feelings should override logic, you're solving your own private trolley problem.

quiz.question: Who first introduced the trolley problem?
quiz.explanation: Philippa Foot introduced the thought experiment in a 1967 paper; Thomson later extended it with the footbridge variant.

quiz.question: Which ethical view says outcomes are what determine right action?
quiz.explanation: Utilitarianism, rooted in Bentham, evaluates acts by the well-being they produce — making 'save five, lose one' the clear choice.

quiz.question: Why do most people pull the lever but refuse to push a stranger off a bridge?
options: Direct physical harm engages emotional brain regions more strongly
The bridge version saves fewer lives
quiz.explanation: Greene's fMRI studies show personal, direct harm activates emotional circuits more than impersonal, lever-based choices.

sources.title: The Problem of Abortion and the Doctrine of the Double Effect
Moral Tribes: Emotion, Reason, and the Gap Between Us and Them
